speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where you're sitting. I'm just turning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Does this look better?

speaker: interviewer
text: --your name. This is a little better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Am I doing all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wonderfully-- wonderfully.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because sometimes like I've-- you know, I-- I've really-- I witnesses something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's great. That's great. Because we want all of that details. That's fine. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two of the interview with Yvette Lennon, on February 12, 1995. You were talking about your sister's wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fiance?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And a few others apart they-- they decided to get married all together. Can you imagine in the backyard there? The rabbi came, and they got married. And-- and then that was almost towards the end now. We're coming towards before we left, you know. In a little while we would leave. And-- and so we-- we were there, living there. And it was-- things were normal. Still, we didn't know what's gonna to happen to us. And then, when we suddenly-- we started to realize that the transports are coming very close. That they decide it was time for my-- for my-- our-- no ours-- just my sister, and me, and her husband, and another group-- the reason that my brother-- that's the one thing that happened. My brother went. They took my brother to work in a Baron Hirsch. It means to play music with the accordion. The Nazis needed somebody to play. And so they took him. They-- they let him stay till the end. He didn't get there yet. He was still in some-- where we were, but they used to go there and play. They made him play and-- and the violinist too, you know. And-- but in the meantime-- now, there was a couple of more transports for us to go. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say transports, were they transports to Baron Hirsch or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Transport to Baron Hirsch. So we-- they-- it was the time that my sister and her husband would leave. But my mother, and father, and my brother, would not leave yet. And I want to tell you, my brother-- my mother meant so much to me. My mother, I couldn't live without my mother, you know. And she, because I was the youngest one, it seems like not that she cared more about me. She was always-- she did so much for me, you know. She loved me very much. Because I was-- my brother was 10 years older than me; my sister, 15. I was the very young one. I came by surprise. They didn't want any more children, I guess. And-- and I was very attached to her-- very close to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was-- it's like here today. Some people couldn't care less about their mothers. I can see-- my mother, if she was sick, I was upset. You're not going to die are you? You know, I was always like this. She said, don't worry. I'm not gonna to die. I said, do-- you're not going to leave me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so when the time came for my brother and-- for-- not my brother-- my sister and all the rest of them and-- to leave-- so my mother was staying. And she would stay. And I would have stayed with her. I had the choice. But I was so stupid. I was so young, and I didn't understand what could happen. And I did-- I-- I thought-- it's-- it's really stupid what I thought-- that I want to see the world. I want to travel. I wanted to go with my sister. I didn't know that we are going in a horrible place like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not even the adults knew. What about a kid? And I always I-- I wanted to travel. I'll say-- I used to sit next to the-- in, you know, in the ocean in my house. And I say, one day, I'm going to go to the America. One day I'm going to go here. And, you know, like I wanted to see what it's like other countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I decided to go with my sister and her husband. My mother says, why are you going with them? Why don't you wait for us? I said, but I'll meet you there. I said, you know-- I-- but I didn't know it. I didn't know this happen. And this stayed with the rest of my life. You know, I felt so bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I left. And-- and she was crying from-- when the day we left. And she was standing there crying, you know, and that. Because I don't know if she knew what's is gonna be pretty bad or not. And-- and so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there anyone there that didn't want to go that Nazis forced to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they went to the mountains. They didn't force. I mean the-- they didn't have to force. They took the names. They knew where we were. It's not like all the countries. My cousin, they didn't get my cousin. They-- I have a friend, they took her mother, her brother, but she-- her piano teacher took her in her house. And she-- she stayed there through the war. And they took her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't know every-- the-- her other aunt didn't make it-- made it. And her mother went to the concentration camp, because this is how we went in France. They didn't have a special place. They tried to find them. They were trying. So in other words, if your sister didn't want to go, she could have gone to the mountains? So she didn't want to go to Baron Hirsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she could-- she could run to the mountain-- hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Run to the mountain, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there they were hiding to other people's houses-- maybe Christian house? I don't know how they did it in France. In Greece, it was like they took us all. There was like 92,000 people from Greece, and only 2,000 came back. Bob just gave me the, you know, the report. Because he was in Greece quite a few times now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So let's just say-- so I left, and I went with my sister and her husband. And there was two more transports for the young. You know, that's when they would come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so anyway, while we were there, we still didn't know what's gonna to happen. We knew that we are going to be transported to Germany. We're going to work there. That's what we thought that we're going to do. We never dreamed what we saw when we came there. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there walls around Baron Hirsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there walls around Baron Hirsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no walls. I don't-- no, I don't think so. But it was an area, a special area that probably they did something so that they don't go. I don't remember that part-- whether they did or not. For everywhere-- well over there too, where we were, there was no walls. And yet everybody went to the camp. We were so stupid. We didn't know. So I mean, after today, I don't know it would be such a cruelty exists. I don't know. I don't know how it existed such cruelty in this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, when we-- then-- so we left. We left, and we stayed a month there. And then I went with my aunt, one of my aunts.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had two sisters. The one sister lived near Baron Hirsch, because her husband was a director of the-- of the mental home. So they took her with us. She had a daughter in France. And she had a daughter with her, and the son, and the crippled one. So there were three of them. And there was my sister, my-- and my sister, her husband, and me. The six of us-- they took us. And the train that they put us in, they put us with the crippled people, because my-- my cousin had no legs. And all the crippled were there. And so we were in the same train. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew it was a train to just Germany? You didn't know to where specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, going to Germany. But the train was so terrible, you know. Like they didn't have seats, that whatever-- they described the trains. That it was like a cattle train-- train. And my-- my brother-in-law, he was-- spoke German so well. So he took over in the train to talk and everything. And he was helping people. But it took us eight days. And we had all our stuff. We know, we were well dressed. We brought our-- all our-- we had money. We had jewelry, because thinking that we need everything, at least the things we could bring. And the eighth day, when we got there, it was the most horrible thing I ever saw. It was 10:00, 11:00 at night. I don't remember the time. But it was very dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, as we got off, there was whistles-- people, you know-- like Nazis running and whistling, and with the dogs, and things saying [NON-ENGLISH]! And we saw. What is this? We were looking. We say, where are we?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, we passed through Budapest and that. We saw the river, you know, the Nile. We saw nice things as we were passing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't-- you weren't locked in the train? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were locked in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think they give us food. Because we had our own things that we brought things. But we were-- you know, we knew it wouldn't be good-- of what we were seeing-- noise, seeing the trains and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, when we got there, that night was the most horrible night I ever spent in-- in my life, you know, I had-- besides these other things came up that were not great. But the fear to see all these people-- what is this? And immediately, the-- these things so fast, like they came. For instance, they-- they separated us. They put the groups like immediately by looking. They won't ask your age, because the-- there's so many people. They don't have time for that. And then you can say any age you want to, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But there they-- they looked at the people. For instance, my aunt was about 55 years old. And probably she had gray hair. And that they'd put her with the older people, because they don't need them for working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they keep us there-- the young ones? It's just because they needed us for work. Otherwise they would send us all to the gas chambers right away. So they put them immediately in group with the ladies-- the men separate-- also older men, and they were not that old. And-- and then they put me, my cousin, my sister, and me-- no, they put me with my aunt. It means that they-- they saw me that I was too young to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put you-- that mean you are for the gas chamber too. So they put me with my aunt. And my sister saw this. They put my cousin with my sister and all the other girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was so much going on there now. So my sister saw that I went with my aunt. And she came and pulled me out. She didn't know there was going to be gas chambers, but she didn't like it. Because she says if they are separating us, I don't know where they are-- everybody going. I want to be with my sister. So she-- she pulled me out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- but when she-- I got so scared when I saw all these things going on. When my sister wasn't looking, I ran to my aunt again. And she pulled. She saw me. And she pulled me out. She say, you stay here with me. I used to hate her! You know, we used to beg-- my sister and me, we-- we-- we-- she was like my teacher, my everything. Like she was my mother. She was-- she loved me, but she was very strict. And I said to my mother, one day, when I get married, I'll go far away from her. And we were hung up together. But she loved me, I realize. She loves me. But at that time, when you are so young, you don't know. But I want to be with my aunt, because I didn't have my mother there. And after we found out that I was in the gas chambers row-- you know, if she didn't pull me out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, now passed, and then they open up the gates. And we saw-- and then we saw a little small barracks here and there. We say, what is this? And then see girls wearing scarves, and they were looking from the window. And we say, who are they? And then suddenly, we heard a Greek talking-- Greek girls. They were there before us. They say, if you have anything, give it to us, give it to us. We said, what are they talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and then suddenly, we passed this-- the barracks. And they put us in a place where what they did, they took all of our luggage right away with their-- already we had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't give anything to the people that asked for stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't, because the Nazi is there! How can we go there? And, you know, we don't know who they were, what they want. You know, we say, what's wrong with them? They look so terrible with the scarves in their heads, no-- no hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, the-- that-- the first thing they took is our suitcases. And they make us give all our jewelry. They had a sack there. We had to take everything we had, the jewelry. And then that was the first step.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second step is they took us to the sauna. Like there they-- they take all your clothes. And then they-- they make you have a shower, and-- as if we were not clean, you know-- a shower. And then they-- they took us-- they make us-- they took all our clothes. And they gave us something horrible things to wear. Like you know, you have seen on TV what they look like with all these green things with the checks, you know-- our thing But at that time, at that moment, they didn't give us that. They gave us some old clothes, like the things that didn't fit you at all. You know, you were wearing things-- especially shoes-- that you give these shoes either either too big or too small. They didn't care. Whatever they had, they give us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took away your shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything! They took us everything. And then we went on this-- when we went into the sauna, I told you, I don't know if this happened before. After the shower, of course, they gave us the clothes. But then what happened next is each one of us has to stand-- to sit with a girl in the sauna, who was also a prisoner, but that was her job. That's the jobs they were given.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they shaved all my hair. And then is when I started crying-- when I saw my hair on the floor. Too much was happening at once, you know. We couldn't believe what's going on. So we were terrified. So and-- and then when I-- I was finished. Oh, after she shaved my hair, she says, what can you do? I said to her, what do you mean what I can do? I say, what can you do? What kind of a work? And-- oh, I say, well, I was in school. I say, I don't know anything. I say, what do you want me to do? He says can't you do anything? Then I say to myself, if I tell her I play music, I think she'll laugh at me. But I said, I have no choice. I say, well, I play the piano and the accordion. I waited a big laugh from her when I saw where we were. Who needs music there? But she didn't say a word. OK. She wrote it down, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next thing, I left. And I saw my sister sitting-- she was finished with another girl-- and my cousin sitting together. And when they saw me coming, they were laughing. They couldn't stop laughing-- because when they saw me with no hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, Michele [NON-ENGLISH]. This my brother's name Michele. He says, that's Michele [NON-ENGLISH], you know? And I said, what are you laughing? Did you see yourselves? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were all sitting there waiting. And then when they told us to go someplace, we went to another barrack, wherever they went. They put this number in the arm-- you know, the number. I don't know if you want to see it today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to show us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You can see it. But I don't know how I'm going to do this. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My little grandson-- oh. I charged some--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just lost the microphone. Let's-- let's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what-- oh, gosh.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. Let's just-- let's show the number. And we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 4-- 43293, which means 43,000-- I was the 43,293rd person.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the 43rd thousand, 293.

speaker: interviewee
text: 2-9-3. And the little-- this sign here, this triangle, means that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The triangle means you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, because there was no-- people who were not Jewish. So it was different--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had different symbols for different people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah-- for all the-- all the kind of people, because there was not just Jewish. Now what do I do? This is the microphone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you went, and you got the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the number. That, you know, that hurt, because each-- each number has so many little, you know, with a needle that they do. They do it like this fast with a needle. And it hurts. You know, it's not just one thing that put a line. That is just like that-- what they do-- like that tattoo, whatever. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hold you down, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hold your arm, yeah. And I say, what next? The next thing is that they give us a soup. This soup looked like-- you know how this like wall paint. And you know wall paint-- you know the white stuff that you stick the wall, you know-- on the wall? Just take the paper--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like glue?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the glue. We didn't eat it. Besides, we were so upset and so scared that we didn't-- we didn't eat it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you taste it, or you just threw it away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't taste it. We were not hungry-- without-- after all these things. And we had already had food in the train. So-- so we-- we didn't have appetite for this at the point.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway-- and suddenly, everybody was changed. It looked so-- because it was such beautiful people. And now it all look so different, you know, in one minute. So that was not our-- our concern. What's going to happen now? That was our concern.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next thing, they brought it-- us into barracks, you know, where-- and you must see-- like that picture you gave me. Is that you who-- we-- no, no. Somebody-- you know, what you see. We had the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means on the wall they had openings for us to sleep. It's all stone. For instance, there is a wall there. And they-- they make a hole, you know. And little-- just enough like from here to there. And there they put the girls enough to sleep-- like five, six girls together. And it's all stone. And don't-- they don't even give you a blanket. So the [NON-ENGLISH]-- it's called the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- you're describing the barracks? Is that what you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks, when we went to the barracks. And when we saw these girls, we realize-- oh, my goodness. What is here? We were saying-- they were saying-- they were saying, it's very bad. It's very bad. And so that night-- by the time we were there, it was 12 o'clock. We went to lie down, and we couldn't sleep. We were all of us next to each other in a little place like that and no blankets. So we were scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I don't know how we felt. I can't even remember. It just was something unreal, you know? Were you in a Greek barracks, or it was just everyone mixed together?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Greeks? No. We were-- they put us in a barracks with who ever comes, you know, whether they're Greeks or Jews. They don't separate the Greeks or not. After they separated us. But that night-- so at 4 o'clock in the morning the whistle started. [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] were also prisoners, but that was their job-- to tell us to get up, because we have to go to work. But we were in quarantine. The say we're in quarantine for a couple of weeks-- I think, the new people who come. But it so happened they-- when we-- when they whistled, and we got dressed fast, we went outdoors. That's what they did every day for the whole time-- that at 4 o'clock, they count you, that you are outside. And they count. There have to be so many people. And if one is missing, we punished. Like we stayed over an hour or so till they find the person. And it's freezing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 4 o'clock in the afternoon or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- in the morning-- very early at night. So we says, that's what they do? We were all shaking and that. And even if-- if somebody didn't-- didn't get away, we still stayed for about 15, 20 minutes there until they count us and all that. And-- and then we go in. And-- and also-- but that next day-- that-- that same day, we were supposed to stay in for two weeks-- I mean, except to go outside to count us. But it happened that that week, that day, they-- one of the girls who was working for the-- that was the worst job that was there. It was carrying bricks and stones. You walk miles. I don't know from here to Hicksville-- I don't know where-- if you know where Hicksville is. It's quite far-- three or four miles from here-- three-- four times in the morning to carry bricks and things like that. Then they give you-- they don't give you nothing in the morning-- just some tea, but tea that's, you know, that doesn't taste like tea-- always water. They give you to drink. That's it. Then in the afternoon, like we did this four times in the morning, going back and forth, back and forth these miles. By they have-- they chose me to be-- there a girl was sick, and they were missing-- she was missing. She was sick. So they-- they chose me to go with them. And I tell you, I will never ever forget that day. This is the [NON-ENGLISH], house commander that they call it. You die. After three months, they die. Because who can do this job with no food in their stomach and going-- do miles and miles. Every time we see them coming back, they-- they had-- they were holding three dead people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that day, I said to the girl, what happens next? He says, we're going to go again. Soon now another three times. So I lay on the ground. I fell asleep. I didn't-- I didn't eat the soup. I didn't-- I was so miserable. I really was scared to death. I say, how am on earth I'm going to do this? So-- so we did the same in the afternoon. When we came back, I said to my sister, if they pick me tomorrow, I'm going to die. I know I'm going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very young and very delicate. I wasn't strong. Like I-- at home, because I had too much homework when I used to go to French school and English school, that I was-- I wasn't strong. You know, like I was working hard to study. And I said to myself, to my sister, I'm going to die. So-- and you can't say no. They picked me. And I wasn't supposed to go. I was supposed to go two weeks later. I don't know where they were going to put us. But something wonderful happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly, a [NON-ENGLISH] came from the main office. And they say-- who speaks-- oh-- who has this number? We don't have names. Numbers-- that was our name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say-- so we look at the number. That was new. I didn't even know what name and number it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she says, I have this number. And my sister say, two girls. I say, who has this other number? My sister, I have it. I say, what do they want from us? I thought, what they're going to do to us? So she says come with me. They never say anything. They let you just wonder. So we went there. And we went to the next camp. There they had the lager A and lager B. And there was a division in between them. You couldn't get from one lager to the other. There was the barbed wires.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, if you go near there and you touch it, you get killed. I saw somebody getting killed by mistake. They put their hand. You know, so in between-- so we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So which lager were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in lager B. And they took us to lager A-- to lager A, all the way around-- the other way around. I don't remember which ones was the A or the B. But we-- we went to this little room. And there was a lady there. She was Polish but not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know at this point the place was called Auschwitz? You knew you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know there. We saw it written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see-- with the view.

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw like-- you can see in the picture that this [GERMAN] something with [GERMAN], you know, work is gonna to make you free-- sure-- the liars. So-- and-- and then we go to this barracks-- to this place. And there was like a little room. And there were six-- four girls sitting down. And they had guitars. And there was a teacher table with music. And the teacher, her name was Tschaikovska. Actually, we found out she was 1/2 nuts, that teacher, because-- that lady-- because she-- she was there a long time. Because was-- she-- she went for political reasons. She was in about four years already. And she was like a nut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and she says-- I said, what does she want? She says, you say you play music? I said, yes. I said what do you play? The accordion.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, here. Play something. She gave me an accordion. So I knew everything by heart. You know, because I-- I used to learn things very easy by heart. So I played for her. She says, good. She says to my sister-- my sister was very smart. She-- she's the one who saved us both. And I save her too. But she was so bright in doing things-- what to do in, you know, in certain situations. She says, I play the accordion too. My sister didn't even touch the accordion. She played the piano. She was a good pianist. It's me and my brother who played the accordion. So-- so she said-- my sister said, I play the accordion, but of course, one hand is like the piano. But we were very fortunate that she was not too smart, the teacher, to know that you use the two hands. So she played with one hand something. There was a piece of music there, and they gave it to her. She read it. She says, good. And then she says, all right. Then a young girl showed up. She had a very good job there. And she-- she was very nice. She was talking to us. She worked there. But I mean, she was a-- a prisoner, but she has been a long time. She had a good job. And she spoke German. See, all the ones who spoke German got the good jobs if they came from Czechoslovakia and that-- but they spoke the language. So she says, OK. Come back tomorrow. So we went back the next day. So it means we don't have to go to work. She called us back to go there to that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sent back to lager B at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We went back every day. We were coming and going. Now we didn't know what she wanted from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after two weeks that we went, they said that they decided-- the camp-- the commandant decided to make an orchestra. I say an orchestra here? I say who needs music here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say-- I mean, they're crazy. They're really-- these people, I say, you would not believe. I can't understand these Nazis. I said, they're not human. I say, when I see a Nazi, I feel this is the devil inside that outfit. This is how I feel. I shiver, you know, when I used to see the Nazis passing-- like little devils in person-- to see such cruelty-- I never saw it to be-- I-- I never could believe it existed. But they were. There he comes in, and he says-- he say to the conductor that-- to the lady-- she was supposed to be our conductor now. She said that we are going to go to the lager B, which is the one-- the other lager-- that we are going to-- they took a barrack, a big barrack. And they had two-- they had two big rooms, tremendous rooms. And in one of the rooms-- it was like two little side rooms for supposedly for the conductor and the block leader. And the other one, the barrack where the girls were going to be, they had bunk beds for a change, you know, like something better than what we saw-- bunk beds like one on top of the other but not together. And-- and they had like a little sort of a kitchenette on the side. And that's-- and that's where-- that's where we're gonna sleep. That they say-- they said that from now on, after the two weeks, that we were going to move to this barrack. And we were gonna to have an orchestra. And every time there will be a new person coming, they will-- they will have an-- you know-- when new musician, they will have them slowly, slowly. The orchestra became 47 people. So we couldn't believe that this is happening. You know, it's like-- now I have to go a bit backwards-- something very interesting that I didn't tell you about my father. My father was a very religious man-- very. He loved God with all his heart. And I knew. And I learned from him by looking at him. You know, he used to take me to the temple every Saturday morning, far away, while we had one synagogue so close, and kept me for the day there. And he says-- and I used to play with the little children outside the building. And I saw when he came home, he always says his prayers before he went to work and when he came back. And I was watching him.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was also a religious person. But from my father, I got the idea to love God by just looking at him, and saying he was such a good man. And while we were in the camp, while we were in Greece, one of the musicians who was in Morocco-- and we went live with him-- he said to my father, tell me, Mr. David. He says, do you still believe that there is a God-- because of what was happening. Do you still believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got so mad, my father. You stop that right now, he said. And I know that even my-- when my father went to the gas chambers, another time, you know-- know what happened, but I know that he even then loved God. I knew my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from this, I used to always say my prayers. And my prayers worked. I say my prayer for little things when I was a kid, because I was punished often, especially by my sister, and now my mother. And-- and I was praying to get out of this punishment. And all my prayer were answered.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, whenever I prayed to God, it was answered. I said, God is my friend. I say, always I'll ask him, and he's going to answer my prayers. Now when I was in the camp, I said please, God. I say, I always answer my prayers-- the little things I asked you. But this-- this time, I want a very big favor. I don't want to die here. Don't let me die here and my sister. He says, I know you won't. You will do it for me. And then I was peaceful. No matter what went wrong, how horrible it was, I'm not going to die here. And that what make me live. Because when I tell you the rest what happened, you can not believe. I, you know-- and that when I wanted to tell you is that when we-- when we went, I was praying. I say, when the music-- and my father-- when-- when I was a kid, when I started to have the-- the accordion lessons, he used to walk me in a tiny little room-- very little-- just for the, you know, the-- for the icebox. We used to have a sort of ice box-- not like here, to put-- the man used to bring the ice-- so little. And it was enough for two persons to sit there. And he used to walk me there and made me there stay two hours to practice my accordion. And then when I came out, I was crying. And he says-- I was crying. But I had to play, because he's listening that I'm studying. But then when I came out, he says, you think--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. We have to change tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You know what? Could you-- could you sit closer to me, maybe?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
